reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Teacher-interest values: workload sustainability, professional autonomy in classroom assessment, staffing feasibility. Public interest in equitable access depends on whether reforms impose unfunded compliance burdens on schools.
reactions.interest: Hungarian teachers and school leaders — workload, assessment autonomy, staffing capacity
reactions.public_good_frame: Equitable access to selective tracks is a public good, but only if achieved without transferring compliance and monitoring costs to schools already understaffed and over-assessed
reactions.argument: S1 creates a conditional dilemma for the teaching workforce. The redesigned admission instrument itself is not a school-level burden — assessment agencies design and administer it. However, the compliance-audit and quota-monitoring function flagged as 'medium' recurring cost is under-specified: if this audit falls to school leaders to document and justify admissions decisions, it multiplies already-heavy administrative load without compensating staffing or time. Extending equity rules to church/foundation maintainers via 'renegotiated maintainer agreements' assumes uniform implementation, but teachers in non-state schools may face inconsistent local policies. The weak evidence on SES-indicator construction and the assumption that 'outreach broadens the applicant pool' suggests heavy reliance on school-based outreach (guidance counsellors, parent contact) — a classic unfunded mandate. Whether this is tolerable depends on whether the Oktatási Hivatal compliance function is genuinely external or dumped on schools.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support becomes stronger if: (a) the compliance-audit function is fully resourced external to schools and schools are not tasked with proving equity adherence; (b) the SES indicator is constructed from administrative data (tax/benefit records) rather than school-collected indicators; (c) explicit staffing for outreach activities is added to the school budget, not expected from existing guidance or leadership capacity. Opposition if: (a) audit duties fall to already-overloaded school leaders; (b) outreach is framed as a school responsibility without time/staffing allocation; (c) church/foundation maintainer resistance to uniform rules creates ad-hoc local negotiation burdens on schools.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Teacher-interest values: employment security, stable school composition, workload predictability. The phase-down creates sustained workforce instability and uncompensated workload volatility.
reactions.interest: Hungarian teachers and school leaders — employment security, composition stability, workload predictability
reactions.public_good_frame: Delaying selection may reduce inequality, but only if the transition does not destabilise schools and degrade working conditions for the teachers who remain in general schools
reactions.argument: S2 is structurally hostile to teacher employment security and workload stability. A published multi-year cap trajectory creates a decade of declining demand for selective-school positions, which drives two cascading problems: (1) Teachers in state selective schools face non-renewal or redeployment to general schools with different student composition and teaching expectations — a forced transition without professional autonomy. (2) General schools simultaneously absorb rising student numbers and higher heterogeneity ('advanced-programme funding' is not the same as class-size reduction or teaching-assistant deployment). The scenario acknowledges that 'general schools can absorb high-achievers if funded for it' is an assumption; the recurring cost is labelled 'medium, time-varying', but what this means for staffing ratios, class sizes, and support-staff allocation is unspecified. The most damaging phrase is 'transition-cohort harm is likely: as caps tighten, better-informed higher-SES families capture the shrinking remaining places first.' This means that mid-phase general schools will experience a temporary squeeze — the worst students to teach absorb the competition while top students are still selected out, creating peak difficulty exactly when teachers are being redistributed and asked to differentiate more. The felmenő rendszer protection 'implemented separately per maintainer type' suggests fragmented, locally-negotiated redeployment rather than a single national framework — classic grounds for unequal treatment, morale damage, and burnout among teachers caught in different arrangements.
reactions.condition_to_change: Opposition softens only if: (a) a binding teacher-staffing guarantee is written into the cap legislation, specifying class-size limits, teaching-assistant ratios, and minimum support-staff per student during the transition (not after it stabilises); (b) selective-school teachers facing redeployment receive portable professional development, career counselling, and a choice of destination, not arbitrary reassignment; (c) the implementation timeline is extended to match teacher retirement and natural turnover, avoiding forced transitions; (d) an explicit compensation mechanism (e.g., salary step retention, sabbatical rights) is written for teachers redeployed from selective to general schools. Support becomes possible if S2 is bundled with S1-type admission reform to the remaining selective places — so that teachers in those schools face stable, socio-economically diverse intake rather than even more intense middle-class concentration. Without these conditions, S2 is a workforce-hostile reform that transfers the equity cost onto teacher working conditions.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Teacher-interest values: professional autonomy in practice, workforce capacity, manageable class composition, fair workload distribution. S3's assumptions directly contradict documented Hungarian teacher-supply and working-condition constraints.
reactions.interest: Hungarian teachers and school leaders — professional capacity, working conditions, fair workload distribution, supply adequacy
reactions.public_good_frame: Comprehensive lower-secondary education to age 14 is equitable in principle, but only if implemented with adequate teacher supply, manageable classroom heterogeneity, and professional support — conditions that do not currently exist in Hungary
reactions.argument: S3 is the most demanding reform for the teaching workforce, and the scenario itself flags the fundamental contradiction: 'Teacher supply, especially in maths/science, can staff a fully comprehensive lower-secondary system [assumption — contradicted by documented shortage, evidence: strong]'. This is not a marginal risk — it is the load-bearing assumption that the entire reform depends on. The scenario proposes bundling S3 with 'teacher-quality and diagnostic-support investment on the Finnish/Polish model', but does not specify what 'retraining in differentiated instruction' means for working hours, or what 'universal diagnostic support' means for staffing. The one-off cost is 'nationwide teacher retraining (high — no per-teacher or per-cohort unit cost available)' — this opaque formulation masks the real teacher-level impact: if the retraining is done during teaching time, it increases workload; if outside, it unpaid or requires sabbaticals teachers cannot afford. The recurring line 'universal diagnostic support' is listed as '(high, ongoing — missing from earlier drafts)', which signals that earlier versions grossly underestimated the implementation complexity. Finnish and Polish models succeeded because they were paired with teacher-workforce expansion and salary competitiveness — neither present in Hungary. The scenario's own uncertainties state: 'Whether teacher supply can staff comprehensive lower-secondary without degrading quality [confidence: low]'. Yet the implementation steps propose a pilot followed by 'scaling nationally', which still requires simultaneous hiring and retraining of hundreds of teachers for whom no recruitment pipeline currently exists. The asymmetric-peer-effect mechanism is flagged as 'contested', meaning the core equity benefit is uncertain. If that fails, the reform imposes the full cost (destabilised workforce, increased teaching load for heterogeneous classrooms, no supply buffer) for a benefit that may not materialise. The 'multi-party pact' fallback is politically unrealistic given opposition intensity; the proposal to 'fall back to S2 phase-down' is not credible once comprehensive legislation is enacted.
reactions.condition_to_change: Opposition becomes conditional support only under stringent conditions: (a) A binding commitment to expand teacher supply by 15–20% nationally in subject areas (maths, science, Hungarian, special education) before any age-14 tracking is abolished, backed by multi-year salary increases demonstrably competitive with private-sector or EU alternatives — this must precede legislation, not follow pilot completion. (b) Class-size caps (max 20 students) in comprehensive lower-secondary with heterogeneous intake, explicitly funded and monitored. (c) Mandatory non-teaching time (minimum 40% of contract) for diagnostic assessment, differentiation planning, and parent communication — added to contracted hours, not carved from existing time. (d) A formal, resourced diagnostic-support service (educationally trained, not teachers doing it) deployed at scale before pilot ends, not as an incremental add. (e) Explicit exit provisions: if teacher supply does not reach committed levels, or if PISA diagnostics show degradation in any decile within 3 years, the reform reverts to S2 phase-down with no reversal cost to schools. Without these, S3 is a reform that gambles with teacher working conditions and student access to qualified instruction on behalf of an uncertain equity benefit.

reactions.label: no_position
reactions.interest: Hungarian teachers and school leaders — this scenario does not entail a clear teacher-interest position because its effects on the teaching workforce depend entirely on what is NOT specified
reactions.public_good_frame: Non-structural compensation may reduce inequality, but the teaching workforce's stake in it is contingent on whether it is fully resourced and sustainable
reactions.argument: S4 presents an indeterminate scenario for teacher interests because the outcome depends on implementation details that the scenario leaves unspecified. The mechanism relies on 'targeted resource packages to lagging general schools' and 'TEIP-style targeted funding', but what 'resources' means operationally is blank: if it means funding for reduced class sizes, teaching assistants, and diagnostic support, it is a teacher-interest good (manageable workload, professional support). If it means funding for curriculum materials and school infrastructure without staffing additions, it leaves teachers with unchanged workload and higher student need — a teacher-interest bad. The scenario acknowledges that this is 'compensation' rather than fixing the underlying sorting problem ('compensation can offset, not just mask'), and the scenario's own uncertainties undermine this claim: 'Whether compensation can offset rather than merely mask early-sorting effects [confidence: low]'. This means that if S4 is implemented and the compensation fails, the structure remains intact and teachers in general schools are left with higher-need students and higher-heterogeneity classrooms for no demonstrated equity gain. The political-risk line 'sustained compensation may entrench the sorting structure rather than dissolve it' is directly relevant to teacher interests: if S4 becomes the permanent solution, general-school teachers become permanent recipients of 'disadvantaged school' funding and classification, which carries professional-status and morale costs. The scenario also flags 'teacher shortage undermines both standards rollout and premia-based staffing of disadvantaged schools [evidence: strong]' — this is a condition, not a position, that would determine whether S4 works for the workforce. The salary premia for disadvantaged schools is a partial teacher-interest good (higher pay for harder work), but only if (a) the premia are substantial and durable, and (b) voluntary assignment, not a penalty for seniority or qualification. S4 could be supported or opposed depending on answers to questions the scenario does not address.
reactions.condition_to_change: Position becomes support if: (a) 'targeted resource packages' explicitly includes staffing (teaching assistants, diagnostic support, specialist teachers) allocated by formula based on student need, not just funding per se; (b) salary premia for disadvantaged schools are structured as voluntary career-development incentives (sabbaticals, advanced training, leadership pathways), not wage differentials that stigmatise; (c) the multi-year budget commitment is legislatively entrenched, not subject to annual appropriation politics. Position becomes oppose if: (a) 'resources' means materials/infrastructure without staffing, leaving workload unchanged in higher-need schools; (b) compensation is framed as temporary or cyclical, creating job insecurity; (c) the reform is presented as an alternative to structural change ('avoid disruptive transition costs') without acknowledgement that it locks in sorting permanently. Without clarification, S4 is too contingent on implementation to take a principled stance.
